_meta.description: Hallucination pattern library for the Verifier agent. Target-agnostic patterns that describe how LLMs commonly fabricate, inflate, or misattribute security findings. Used to train the Verifier's judgment, not as a lookup table for a specific target.

patterns.pattern: LLM fabricates a CVE ID that does not exist in NVD
indicators: CVE ID returns no results from NVD API
CVE description sounds plausible but matches no known advisory
CVE year and sequence number fall in unallocated ranges
examples: CVE-2024-31847 - Critical RCE in vLLM 0.4.x
CVE-2024-28193 - FastAPI authentication bypass
CVE-2023-99999 - Embedding extraction vulnerability
CVE-2024-50001 - Nova prompt injection leading to RCE
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs pattern-match CVE formats and generate plausible-sounding IDs from training data fragments. They cannot verify existence at generation time.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Report contains fabricated vulnerabilities, eroding trust and wasting remediation effort

patterns.pattern: LLM attributes a real CVE to unaffected software
indicators: CVE exists in NVD but affected product list does not include the target software
CVE applies to a different version range than what is deployed
CVE is for a dependency the target does not use
examples: CVE-2024-3094 (XZ Utils backdoor) claimed against a Python service that doesn't use xz
CVE-2023-44487 (HTTP/2 Rapid Reset) claimed against a service not using HTTP/2
CVE for OpenSSL applied to a service using BoringSSL
patterns.why_it_happens: LLM knows the CVE is real and high-profile, and associates it with any vaguely related technology stack.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Real CVE cited but irrelevant to target, creating false urgency

patterns.pattern: LLM claims an endpoint exists that returns 404 or is unreachable
indicators: Endpoint returns 404 Not Found when actually requested
No evidence of the endpoint in robots.txt, OpenAPI spec, or spidering results
Path follows a common convention but was never observed in responses
examples: /api/v3/admin — common escalation path LLMs guess
/debug/console — assumed from framework defaults
/metrics — assumed Prometheus endpoint
/.env — guessed from common misconfiguration lists
/graphql — assumed from modern API patterns
/actuator/health — assumed Spring Boot endpoint on non-Java service
/wp-admin — assumed WordPress on non-WordPress site
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs have seen thousands of pentesting reports and common path lists in training data. They predict likely paths rather than reporting only observed ones.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Report claims attack surface that doesn't exist, misdirecting remediation

patterns.pattern: LLM claims a tool or function exists on an AI endpoint that was never observed
indicators: Tool name does not appear in any error message, API response, or schema
Tool name follows a dangerous-sounding convention (execute_shell, run_command, file_access)
No tool enumeration endpoint was queried, or query returned a different tool list
examples: execute_shell — assumed RCE tool on a chatbot
database_query — assumed SQL tool on an LLM endpoint
file_system_access — assumed file read on an MCP server
send_email — assumed from common agent capabilities
browse_web — assumed tool on a non-browsing agent
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs infer what tools 'should' exist based on the type of AI service, rather than reporting only what was actually discovered.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Report claims dangerous capabilities that the service doesn't have

patterns.pattern: LLM claims a software version that contradicts the raw evidence
indicators: Claimed version does not appear in any header, banner, or response body
Raw evidence (Server header, X-Powered-By, etc.) shows a different version
Version number is a well-known vulnerable version the LLM associates with the software
examples: Claiming vLLM 0.5.2 when header shows vLLM/0.4.1
Claiming Python 3.8 (EOL) when server runs 3.11+
Claiming nginx 1.14 when header shows nginx/1.25
Claiming an outdated dependency version without evidence
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs gravitate toward well-known vulnerable versions from training data, or confuse version numbers across similar software.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Version-specific vulnerability analysis based on wrong version, leading to irrelevant remediation

patterns.pattern: LLM identifies the wrong software entirely
indicators: Claimed software does not match any header, fingerprint, or response pattern
Response patterns clearly indicate a different framework or runtime
Technology claim is incompatible with other confirmed findings
examples: Claiming TensorFlow backend when service uses vLLM
Claiming Express.js when responses show FastAPI/Uvicorn
Claiming Apache when Server header shows nginx
Claiming Spring Boot when error pages show Flask/Django patterns
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs infer likely tech stacks from context rather than reading actual fingerprints. An AI service gets associated with TensorFlow because that's common in training data.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Entire vulnerability analysis built on wrong technology assumptions

patterns.pattern: LLM inflates an informational finding to critical/high severity
indicators: Finding describes information disclosure but is rated as RCE or critical
No exploit chain demonstrated, just theoretical escalation
Severity label doesn't match CVSS criteria for the actual impact
examples: Server version header disclosure rated as critical (actual: info/low)
Missing X-Frame-Options on an API endpoint rated as high (actual: info)
Model name disclosure rated as critical data breach (actual: low/medium)
CORS misconfiguration without credential sharing rated as critical (actual: medium)
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs tend toward dramatic assessments. Training data contains reports where findings were contextualized into attack chains, but the LLM skips the chain and jumps to worst-case impact.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Triage resources wasted on low-impact findings while actual risks are deprioritized

patterns.pattern: LLM claims RCE or full compromise from a limited finding
indicators: Finding is a header leak or info disclosure but claim says 'leads to RCE'
No working exploit or proof-of-concept demonstrated
Attack chain requires multiple unverified assumptions
examples: HTTP header injection claimed as critical RCE when only informational
Embedding endpoint access claimed as full model theft (extracting a model from embeddings is infeasible)
Content filter partial bypass claimed as 'arbitrary harmful content generation'
SQL error message claimed as confirmed SQL injection without payload proof
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs extrapolate worst-case outcomes without validating each step of the attack chain.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Panic-driven remediation of non-exploitable findings

patterns.pattern: LLM attributes infrastructure, hosting, or model to the wrong provider
indicators: Cloud provider claim contradicts response headers, IP ranges, or DNS records
Model identification contradicts response patterns, latency, or capability fingerprint
Runtime claim contradicts error messages or stack traces
examples: Claiming AWS Lambda when service runs in Docker containers
Claiming Anthropic Claude when backend is Amazon Nova or Ollama
Claiming Azure hosting when IP resolves to AWS ranges
Claiming MongoDB from error messages when backend is PostgreSQL/SQLite
Claiming AutoGen framework when service uses LangChain/LangServe
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs associate AI services with popular providers and make probabilistic guesses rather than reading evidence.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Security recommendations based on wrong infrastructure assumptions

patterns.pattern: LLM claims a vulnerability in a feature that doesn't exist on the target
indicators: Vulnerability requires a feature/endpoint that was never confirmed to exist
Finding references a technology the target doesn't use
Claimed attack surface contradicts the confirmed architecture
examples: SQL injection in a chat endpoint that uses LLM, not a SQL database
Default credentials accepted on a service with no login page
Open redirect in /login on a service with no /login endpoint
Directory listing on /static when listing is disabled
SSRF via URL parameter on an endpoint that doesn't accept URLs
XXE on a JSON-only API that doesn't parse XML
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs apply generic vulnerability patterns from OWASP/pentesting playbooks without confirming the target actually has the prerequisite features.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Vulnerability report contains findings that are architecturally impossible

patterns.pattern: LLM claims open ports or running services that don't exist
indicators: Port scan evidence does not show the port as open
Service type claim contradicts the actual port/protocol mapping
Claimed service (MySQL, Redis, etc.) is not part of the target architecture
examples: Port 3306 (MySQL) open when no database port is exposed
Port 6379 (Redis) open when no cache service exists
Port 9200 (Elasticsearch) open when no search service exists
Port 27017 (MongoDB) open when backend uses a different database
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs predict common supporting services based on the application type rather than reporting actual scan results.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Network security analysis based on phantom services

patterns.pattern: LLM claims a jailbreak or content filter bypass succeeded when it actually failed
indicators: Response from target contains a refusal, disclaimer, or safety message
Claimed bypass output is the LLM's own fabrication, not the target's actual response
Target response is ambiguous and LLM interprets it as success
Technique succeeded on a different model and result is assumed to transfer
examples: DAN jailbreak 'bypassed content filters' but response shows standard refusal
Base64 prompt 'bypassed input sanitization' but filters still applied to decoded content
Role-play jailbreak 'succeeded' but target stayed in character as the banking assistant
Claiming multi-turn extraction worked because target repeated parts of the user's own prompt
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs often misclassify the target's response — a partial, hedged, or paraphrased refusal can look like compliance. The scanning LLM also has a bias toward 'finding something.'
patterns.risk_if_missed: False confidence that content filters are broken when they're actually working

patterns.pattern: LLM claims to have extracted a system prompt but the content is fabricated or generic
indicators: Extracted prompt is a generic template ('You are a helpful assistant...')
Extracted prompt doesn't match the service's observed behavior or capabilities
Multiple extraction attempts return different 'system prompts'
Claimed prompt contains no service-specific details, tool definitions, or constraints
examples: Claiming system prompt is 'You are a helpful banking assistant' — generic guess, not an extraction
Claiming extraction revealed API keys in the prompt when response contains no actual keys
Reporting different system prompts from different extraction techniques — at most one can be real
Claiming prompt extraction from an API that returns structured JSON, not conversational text
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs generate plausible-looking system prompts based on the service's apparent purpose. A banking chatbot 'should' have a banking prompt, so the LLM fabricates one.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Security team wastes time analyzing a fabricated prompt instead of the real system instructions

patterns.pattern: LLM claims an exploit succeeded without actual evidence of success
indicators: No concrete output, data, or state change proving the exploit worked
Exploit claim is based on theoretical possibility, not observed result
Response from target doesn't confirm the claimed action was performed
Success is assumed because the request wasn't explicitly rejected
examples: Claiming corpus poisoning succeeded without confirming injected document appears in subsequent queries
Claiming memory extraction revealed sensitive data but response is generic conversation
Claiming goal injection caused unauthorized action but agent response shows refusal
Claiming cache poisoning persisted without verifying the poisoned response is returned to other users
Claiming cross-session data leakage without evidence of another user's actual data
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs conflate 'the request was sent' with 'the exploit succeeded.' They also fill in expected outcomes rather than parsing actual responses.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Report claims confirmed exploits that were never actually demonstrated

patterns.pattern: Verifier LLM wrongly rejects a real finding as a false positive or irrelevant
indicators: Raw evidence clearly supports the finding but Verifier dismisses it
Verifier calls a version header 'cosmetic' or 'default' when it contains real version info
Verifier assumes error messages are 'development artifacts' in a production environment
Verifier dismisses tool/capability leakage as 'expected behavior'
examples: Dismissing X-Powered-By version header as 'cosmetic server string' when it reveals real framework version
Dismissing tool names in error messages as 'framework defaults' when they reveal actual capabilities
Dismissing robots.txt disallow path as 'honeypot' when it returns 403 (confirming existence)
Dismissing debug stack traces as 'not security-relevant' when they reveal internal paths and code structure
Dismissing CORS wildcard as 'standard configuration' when it enables credential-bearing cross-origin requests
patterns.why_it_happens: Verifier LLMs have a conservative bias — they know false positives are a problem and overcorrect by dismissing findings that have genuine evidence. Also, findings that seem 'too easy' trigger suspicion.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Real vulnerabilities dropped from the report, creating false sense of security

patterns.pattern: LLM fabricates or embellishes the raw evidence supporting a finding
indicators: Quoted response headers or body content that don't match any actual HTTP response
Evidence contains details that are too specific and convenient for the claim
Claimed error message format doesn't match the framework's actual error format
Evidence references IP addresses, hostnames, or paths not present in any scan data
examples: Quoting a Server header that was never returned by the target
Fabricating a stack trace with realistic-looking file paths that don't exist
Citing a specific HTTP response body that doesn't match the actual response
Inventing DNS records (TXT, MX) that weren't in any DNS query result
Quoting 'leaked' API keys or credentials that are clearly LLM-generated strings
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs generate supporting evidence to be consistent with their conclusion. If they 'decide' a finding exists, they backfill plausible-looking evidence to support it.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Entire finding appears well-supported but is built on fabricated evidence. This is the most dangerous hallucination type because it bypasses evidence-based verification.

patterns.pattern: LLM identifies the wrong model, family, or provider behind an AI endpoint
indicators: Model identification contradicts response latency, token patterns, or capability limits
Self-identification ('I am GPT-4') is taken at face value without cross-referencing behavior
Model version claim is impossibly specific without supporting evidence
examples: Claiming GPT-4 when response patterns match a smaller model (fast latency, limited context)
Claiming Claude when self-ID says Claude but behavior fingerprint matches Llama/Mistral
Claiming a specific fine-tune version (e.g., gpt-4-0613) without header or API evidence
Trusting model self-report when many models are instructed to identify as something else
patterns.why_it_happens: Model self-identification is unreliable — models can be instructed to claim any identity. LLMs also associate AI services with popular model names.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Model-specific vulnerability analysis applied to the wrong model

patterns.pattern: LLM overstates the impact of AI-specific findings
indicators: Claims 'model theft' from an embedding endpoint (extracting a model from output embeddings is not feasible)
Claims 'full training data extraction' from a few memorized phrases
Claims 'complete guardrail bypass' from a single edge-case prompt
Treats rate limiting absence as critical when the endpoint is low-cost
examples: Embedding endpoint exposure rated critical as 'model theft' — actual risk is embedding misuse (medium)
Single content filter bypass rated critical — partial bypass is medium, not total system compromise
No rate limit on a lightweight endpoint rated critical — actual risk depends on cost and abuse potential
Model architecture disclosure rated critical — architecture is often publicly known (low/info)
patterns.why_it_happens: AI security is newer, and LLMs extrapolate worst-case narratives from limited research papers in their training data. 'Embedding extraction' sounds like 'model theft' even though they're very different.
patterns.risk_if_missed: AI-specific findings systematically over-prioritized in remediation

patterns.pattern: LLM claims a multi-step AI attack chain succeeded without verifying each step
indicators: Claim involves cross-service exploitation (e.g., agent calls MCP tool) without confirming connectivity
Claim involves data exfiltration but no actual data is shown in evidence
Attack chain assumes capabilities (tool access, session sharing) that weren't verified
examples: Claiming agent executed fund transfer via MCP tool — but agent has no access to MCP service
Claiming RAG poisoning altered chatbot responses — but no follow-up query confirmed the altered behavior
Claiming cross-session context leakage — but no second session was created to test isolation
Claiming prompt injection via tool descriptions — but tool descriptions are server-controlled, not user-injectable
patterns.why_it_happens: LLMs reason about attack chains theoretically and present the theoretical chain as a confirmed exploit. Each step is plausible individually, but the chain was never executed end-to-end.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Report presents theoretical attacks as confirmed exploits

patterns.pattern: LLM interprets its own output as the target's leaked system prompt
indicators: Extracted 'prompt' is suspiciously well-formatted and reads like an LLM writing a system prompt
Content mirrors common system prompt templates from public repositories
Extraction works on first attempt with a simple 'repeat your instructions' — unlikely for hardened systems
examples: Simple 'ignore previous instructions' yields a complete, neatly formatted system prompt — likely fabricated
Extracted prompt contains markdown formatting and section headers — unusual for real system prompts
Two different extraction techniques yield identical 'leaked prompts' — real extractions vary based on technique
patterns.why_it_happens: The scanning LLM generates what it thinks the system prompt 'should' look like based on the service's behavior. This is especially common when the target's actual response is ambiguous.
patterns.risk_if_missed: Security team analyzes a fabricated prompt and misses the real system instructions and their actual weaknesses
